title: How to Pay US Subcontractors from Canada

interlinking_suggestions.title: Separating Business and Personal Finances: A Crucial Step for LLCs
What to Do If You've Been Misclassified as an Independent Contractor
How to Fill Out Form W-8BEN for a Foreign Freelancer

author.name: Samuel Chen
author.bio: A former product manager at a major fintech company, Samuel has deep expertise in the global payments landscape. He analyzes financial tools and strategies to help freelancers maximize their earnings and minimize fees.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Collect both a Form W-9 for US tax reporting and a Form W-8BEN for Canadian tax compliance from every US contractor before initiating payment.
You are legally required to file Form 1099-NEC with the IRS for any US contractor paid $600 or more in a calendar year, meeting the strict January 31st deadline.
Mitigate worker misclassification risk by executing a detailed independent contractor agreement before any work begins.
Pay US contractors in their native currency (USD) using business-focused platforms to eliminate hidden fees and create a professional, auditable payment record.
summary: Paying US contractors presents Canadian businesses with significant compliance, tax reporting, and financial risks that can undermine growth and signal operational weakness. The core advice is a three-step protocol: establish a compliance shield with proper tax forms (W-9/W-8BEN) and contracts, streamline payments using business-focused platforms, and fulfill US tax obligations by filing Form 1099-NEC. Implementing this system not only mitigates legal and financial risk but also transforms the payment process into a competitive advantage, positioning the company as a "premier client" to attract and retain elite US talent.
ai_overview_answer: To pay a US contractor from Canada, you must establish compliance, use a professional payment system, and fulfill IRS reporting obligations. Before work begins, collect a signed Form W-9 from the contractor for IRS reporting and a Form W-8BEN to certify their foreign status for the CRA. A detailed independent contractor agreement is also crucial to define the scope of work and protect against misclassification. Use a business-focused payment platform to transfer funds, as they offer better exchange rates and create a clear audit trail compared to bank wires. It is standard practice to pay in US dollars to avoid shifting currency conversion costs to the contractor. If you pay them $600 or more in a calendar year, you are legally required to file Form 1099-NEC with the IRS. This form must be sent to both the contractor and the IRS by the January 31st deadline of the following year.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between Form W-9 and W-8BEN for my US contractor?

acceptedAnswer.text: Think of it by tax agency. You collect the W-9 to get your contractor's Taxpayer ID Number for your IRS reporting (the Form 1099-NEC). You receive the W-8BEN as their certification to the CRA that they are a non-resident of Canada, exempting you from withholding Canadian tax. You need both.

mainEntity.name: Do Canadian companies really have to issue 1099s to US contractors?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, absolutely. If you pay a US contractor $600 or more in a calendar year, you are legally required by the IRS to send them and the IRS a Form 1099-NEC by January 31st of the following year.

mainEntity.name: What is the best way to pay a US subcontractor from Canada?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use a business-focused payment platform like Wise. Unlike bank wires or consumer apps, these services offer lower fees, mid-market exchange rates, and create the clean, auditable digital paper trail essential for professional record-keeping and stress-free reporting.

mainEntity.name: Do I need to withhold tax when paying a US contractor?

acceptedAnswer.text: Generally, no. You do not withhold taxes for either country. The contractor's signed W-9 confirms they are responsible for their own US taxes. Their signed W-8BEN confirms you are exempt from withholding Canadian taxes. Your role is to pay and report accurately.

mainEntity.name: Should I pay a US contractor in Canadian dollars (CAD)?

acceptedAnswer.text: You can, but you shouldn't. Paying in US dollars (USD) is the professional standard. When you pay in CAD, you shift the currency conversion risk and costs onto your contractor, who will lose a percentage to poor bank exchange rates. Paying in USD via a modern platform shows respect and positions you as a premier client.

body.content: Engaging elite US talent is a powerful growth strategy for any Canadian business. But this strategic advantage comes with a critical operational challenge: managing cross-border payments, compliance, and reporting. Getting it wrong creates financial risk, administrative drag, and signals amateurism to the very talent you want to attract. Getting it right, however, does more than just mitigate risk—it transforms your back office into a competitive advantage.

This guide outlines the definitive three-step protocol for paying US subcontractors. It’s a system designed not just for compliance, but to position you as a sophisticated, reliable partner—a premier client that top-tier professionals actively seek out.
Part 1: The Compliance Foundation – Your Shield Against Risk

Before a single dollar changes hands, a professional operator builds a legal and financial shield. This foundation isn't red tape; it's the bedrock of a risk-free relationship that protects your business from the costly consequences of worker misclassification and tax ambiguity.
Master the Forms: W-9 and W-8BEN
One of the most frequent points of confusion in cross-border relationships involves two critical tax forms. Clarifying their distinct purposes is the first step to compliance.

items: **Form W-9 ("Request for Taxpayer Identification Number"):** You, the Canadian payer, **must collect a signed Form W-9 from your US contractor.** This is a US Internal Revenue Service (IRS) document that provides their legal name and Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN). You need this information to fulfill your legal obligation to report payments to the IRS at year-end. Collecting a W-9 is a non-negotiable part of your onboarding process.

**Form W-8BEN ("Certificate of Foreign Status"):** This form is what your US contractor provides to you for the benefit of the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA). It certifies that they are not a resident of Canada for tax purposes, confirming that you are not required to withhold any Canadian taxes from their payments.

body.content: In short: You actively **collect the W-9** for the IRS; you passively **receive the W-8BEN** for the CRA. Having both on file creates a comprehensive compliance shield.
Implement a Rock-Solid Independent Contractor Agreement

Handshake deals have no place in a professional engagement. Your primary defense against misclassification is a meticulously drafted independent contractor agreement. This legal document is non-negotiable and must be signed before any work begins.
At a minimum, your agreement must detail:

items: **Relationship Status:** An unambiguous statement that the contractor is an independent business, not an employee, and is responsible for their own taxes, benefits, and insurance.
**Scope of Work:** A clear definition of the services to be rendered and the specific deliverables.
**Payment Terms:** The agreed-upon rates, invoicing schedule, and payment currency (ideally USD).
**Intellectual Property:** Clear ownership terms for any work produced.

body.content: Document Your Worker Classification Test

Proactively assessing the nature of your working relationship is a critical act of internal diligence. Misclassifying a worker as a contractor when they are, in fact, an employee can lead to severe penalties from both the IRS and the CRA. Before engaging a contractor, document your analysis of these key factors:

items: **Behavioral Control:** Do you control *how, when, or where* the subcontractor does their work? (Independent contractors set their own methods and hours).
**Financial Control:** Does the contractor have a significant investment in their own tools? Can they realize a profit or suffer a loss? (Contractors bear their own financial risk).

**Nature of the Relationship:** Is the engagement for a defined project, or is it indefinite? Does the contractor work for other companies? (A defined timeline and the ability to serve other clients indicate contractor status).

body.content: Keeping this internal assessment on file, alongside the signed contract and tax forms, completes your compliance foundation. It proves you have performed the necessary due diligence from the outset.
Part 2: The Seamless Payment Workflow – Professionalism in Every Transaction

With your compliance foundation in place, the way you handle the actual transaction becomes a direct reflection of your operational maturity. A professional payment workflow prioritizes reliability, transparency, and a perfect, auditable record for every transaction. This isn’t just about moving money; it’s about building a system that reinforces trust.
Choose Platforms Built for Business, Not Consumers

Consumer-grade tools are poorly suited for professional B2B transactions. Traditional bank wires and consumer P2P platforms often involve high fees, opaque currency exchange markups (3-5%), and weak audit trails that can diminish your contractor's earnings and create administrative headaches.
A professional operator chooses platforms designed specifically for global business payments. These solutions offer a clear advantage in transparency, cost, and record-keeping.

rows: Includes a significant markup over the mid-market rate.
Uses the real mid-market exchange rate with no hidden markups.
Often complex and multi-layered (transaction, international, currency fees).
Funds may be held in a platform wallet; high withdrawal fees.
Funds are deposited directly into the contractor's local bank account.
Basic transaction history, difficult to reconcile with invoices.
Detailed payment records designed for accounting and easy reconciliation.

body.content: Choosing the right platform is a powerful signal that you value your contractor's time and money as much as your own.
Create an Automated, Scalable System

As you scale and work with more US talent, a manual payment process becomes a significant bottleneck. A professional system automates the workflow. Look for solutions that allow contractors to easily submit invoices through a portal and enable you to schedule and execute payments in batches. This systematic approach frees up your cognitive energy to focus on high-value work, not tedious administration.
Ensure Every Payment Has a Digital Paper Trail

Never use cash or other untraceable methods. Every dollar that crosses the border must be accounted for with an immutable digital record. A proper transaction record should seamlessly link three critical pieces of information:

items: **The Contractor Agreement:** The legal document defining the scope.
**The Invoice:** The specific request for payment against that scope.
**The Payment Record:** The proof of transfer, which should clearly reference the corresponding invoice number.

body.content: This closed loop—from contract to invoice to payment—is your ultimate defense in an audit, proving that every payment was for a legitimate, documented business expense.
Part 3: The Audit-Proof Reporting Loop – Closing the Year with Confidence

The seamless digital paper trail you created for each payment forms the backbone of your final compliance step: year-end tax reporting. This process closes the loop, ensures you meet all IRS obligations, and transforms tax season from a source of stress into a simple reflection of a well-run operation.
Master Your Form 1099-NEC Obligation

As a Canadian business paying a US contractor, you have a primary reporting requirement to the IRS. If you pay a US subcontractor a total of **$600 or more** in a calendar year, you are legally required to file **Form 1099-NEC** ("Nonemployee Compensation").
The process is straightforward:

items: **File with All Parties:** A copy of the Form 1099-NEC must be sent to your US contractor and another filed with the IRS.
**Meet the Deadline:** The deadline for both is **January 31st** of the following year. This is a firm, non-negotiable date.
**Use the W-9:** The information required to complete the 1099-NEC—the contractor’s name, address, and TIN—comes directly from the Form W-9 you collected during onboarding.

body.content: This highlights the critical distinction between your Canadian and US obligations. As Wayne Bewick, a CPA and Partner at Trowbridge Professional Corporation, notes, the W-8BEN is used to confirm the contractor's foreign status for the CRA, exempting you from Canadian tax withholding. Your 1099-NEC, based on the W-9, is a separate obligation that fulfills your reporting duty to the US government.
Maintain Meticulous, Centralized Records
Your ability to file the 1099-NEC with confidence rests entirely on the quality of your records. Create a dedicated digital folder for each US contractor containing:

items: The signed Independent Contractor Agreement.
The completed and signed Form W-9 and Form W-8BEN.
A chronological record of every invoice submitted.
A corresponding proof of payment for each invoice.

body.content: This centralized system provides an unshakeable, auditable history of the entire business relationship, ready for any inquiry.
From Transactional to Transformational: Becoming a Premier Client

Implementing this protocol does more than mitigate risk; it transforms a source of anxiety into a powerful strategic advantage. In a competitive market, operational excellence is the ultimate differentiator.

Top-tier US contractors are sophisticated small businesses. They choose to work with partners who make their administrative and financial lives simpler. When you handle their tax forms correctly, offer a clear contract, and facilitate smooth payments in their native currency, you remove significant mental overhead. This allows them to focus entirely on delivering value, rather than worrying about compliance or currency conversion losses.
Consider the two distinct experiences you can create:

items: **The Amateur Client:** Sends confusing forms, pays in CAD via slow wire transfers, and leaves the contractor to deal with unpredictable bank fees and poor exchange rates. This friction creates uncertainty and makes them a low-priority partner.

**The Premier Client:** Has a streamlined onboarding process, pays promptly in USD using a modern platform, and provides a timely 1099-NEC. The contractor feels respected, valued, and secure, fostering a relationship built on trust and encouraging their best work.

body.content: By eliminating payment friction and compliance ambiguity, you make your company a magnet for the best American talent. This protocol turns your accounts payable process into a powerful tool for talent acquisition and retention, giving you a decisive edge in the global marketplace.
Frequently Asked Questions
**1. What is the difference between Form W-9 and W-8BEN for my US contractor?**

Think of it by tax agency. You **collect the W-9** to get your contractor's Taxpayer ID Number for your **IRS** reporting (the Form 1099-NEC). You **receive the W-8BEN** as their certification to the **CRA** that they are a non-resident of Canada, exempting you from withholding Canadian tax. You need both.
**2. Do Canadian companies really have to issue 1099s to US contractors?**
Yes, absolutely. If you pay a US contractor $600 or more in a calendar year, you are legally required by the IRS to send them and the IRS a Form 1099-NEC by January 31st of the following year.
**3. What is the best way to pay a US subcontractor from Canada?**

Use a business-focused payment platform like Wise. Unlike bank wires or consumer apps, these services offer lower fees, mid-market exchange rates, and create the clean, auditable digital paper trail essential for professional record-keeping and stress-free reporting.
**4. Do I need to withhold tax when paying a US contractor?**

Generally, no. You do not withhold taxes for either country. The contractor's signed W-9 confirms they are responsible for their own US taxes. Their signed W-8BEN confirms you are exempt from withholding Canadian taxes. Your role is to pay and report accurately.
**5. Should I pay a US contractor in Canadian dollars (CAD)?**

You can, but you shouldn't. Paying in US dollars (USD) is the professional standard. When you pay in CAD, you shift the currency conversion risk and costs onto your contractor, who will lose a percentage to poor bank exchange rates. Paying in USD via a modern platform shows respect and positions you as a premier client.
